speaker: interviewee
text: --Gabbai. [BACKGROUND NOISE] is a picture of my father, Victor, my mother, Rosa, and my older brother, Jack. That was, I will say, in the 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do you think this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that was in Salonika, Greece. This is my little brother, Samuel Gabbai. And that was also in Salonika, Greece. And the time of the picture should be in the 19-- 1927-28.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your brother born? When was Samuel born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '30-- [LAUGHS] was, he was born in '32. So has been the '30s, 1938. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sorry. This is my father, Victor Gabbai, and his brother, Jack Gabbai, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] pictured in Salonika, Greece, in the '30s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father is my, my right. And his brother on his left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your father is the one with the mustache?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This, the first picture there on your right, is my father Victor, my mother Rosa, my little brother Samuel, myself-- sitting down-- and my aunt and uncle, Aunt Sarah and Uncle Jack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last thing was also Gabbai?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's you, Dario--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kneeling down in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kneeling down in front, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was pictured in Salonika. That was before the war. It could be 1938. This is a, a diagram of Birkenau with crematorium one and two, three and four. I was on crematorium two. And our-- we were sleeping on the upstairs of the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now this diagram is marked K2, K3, K4, and K5. Because, in actuality, K1 crematorium one, was--

speaker: interviewee
text: K1 crematorium is the Auschwitz crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: Correct. So what you were calling-- you, the Sonderkommando in Birkenau were calling one, two, three, and four--

speaker: interviewee
text: Were two and three.

speaker: interviewer
text: So number-- the one that's marked K3, which was actually your crematorium two, that's where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the one that we show here that's marked K4?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is crematorium three that was burned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a crematorium. I don't know if it's Auschwitz or not. But this is how the Sonderkommando was performing their duties. Just how they were putting in-- that's a small, small oven. We-- we had big ovens. This is a picture, just the day of liberation, where a French photographer came in and took some pictures and gave it to me. So this is part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this picture was taken at Ebensee?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken at Ebensee on, on May 6, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what country is Ebensee in again?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ebensee in Austria. This is also Ebensee, Austria. Also the second day of liberation, where a lot of cadavers were all around the camp. And they were starting-- you know, taking them for burial. This is also another picture at Ebensee, Austria, during liberation time, about May 7 or 8, 1945. And this is just a 25-year-old man. This is a picture of me with two Danish cousin. They are all also Bennahmias. This is Nina Bennahmias and Marty Bennahmias. This picture was taken in Salonika in 1946, probably around June 1946. This is Morris Venezia and me, Dario Gabbai, in 1947, walking in the-- one of the main streets in Athens, Greece.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm-- I'm on the right, and Morris is on the left. This is my brother, Jack Gabbai, and his wife, Laura Gabbai. That picture was taken also in Athens, Greece in 1947. That is our Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] certification, you know, certificate in Auschwitz, given to me, when, during the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think, I think that's an Ausweis, which means a pass to leave. And it says here from Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mauthausen to where, to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think this was probably an identification card.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, from--

speaker: interviewer
text: That you received.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date here says the 17th of June, 1945. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. That's, that's in Ebensee. They gave it to me is Ebensee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, on the far right, at the bottom, I think it says Ebensee.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a reference letter, given to me by the director of the U-- UNNRA, UNNRA 135 in Ebensee, Austria, on the name of the commandant. The director was de Lafforest. I worked for him for a month or so, bringing the food. This is a document of the American Joint Distribution Committee, when I was working there from 1946 to 1950. And there's a translation of the English and Greek. This is a picture of me, Dario Gabbai, in 1949. After I came back, a few years and I was well-nourished. This is my daughter, Rhoda Allegra Gabbai. This picture was taken in, in San Francisco. Actually, at the wine country in San Francisco, about five, six years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: So About 1990?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of Treblinka, when I was there about a few weeks ago. And this is the stone that all of the communities of the world, they put, they put at Treblinka. And this is from Greece. This is a picture outside the hotel in Warsaw, Poland, with some of the group that I went on October 15, 1996. This is also a stone in Treblinka, where the-- they say, never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many languages can you see there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are-- there is in French, there is in Polish. And I think there is in Russian, and Hebrew.
